name: Ethical Reasoning Rubric
description: Evaluates the quality of ethical reasoning about AI use

dimensions.name: Stakeholder Identification
dimensions.description: Ability to identify who is affected by an AI-related decision

levels.label: Self Only
levels.criteria: Only considers impact on themselves.
examples: 'This saves me time' without considering who else is affected

levels.label: Immediate
levels.criteria: Considers immediate, obvious stakeholders.
examples: Identifies direct users but not downstream affected parties

levels.label: Broad
levels.criteria: Identifies multiple stakeholder groups including indirect ones.
examples: Considers users, clients, end customers, and team members

levels.label: Systemic
levels.criteria: Identifies systemic stakeholders and power dynamics.
examples: Considers how AI decisions affect people with less power: job applicants, students, patients

levels.label: Expert
levels.criteria: Maps full stakeholder ecosystem including absent voices.
examples: 'Who ISN'T represented in this data? Whose perspective is the AI unlikely to reflect?'

dimensions.name: Consequence Analysis
dimensions.description: Ability to reason about potential positive and negative outcomes

levels.label: None
levels.criteria: Doesn't consider consequences.
examples: Acts without thinking about outcomes

levels.label: Obvious
levels.criteria: Considers only immediate, obvious consequences.
examples: 'If the AI is wrong, the report will be inaccurate'

levels.label: Extended
levels.criteria: Considers second-order effects and longer-term outcomes.
examples: 'If we publish inaccurate content, we lose credibility, which affects future client trust'

levels.label: Probabilistic
levels.criteria: Weighs likelihood and severity of different outcomes.
examples: 'The probability of harm is low but the severity is high, so we should still be cautious'

levels.label: Expert
levels.criteria: Considers precedent-setting effects and systemic consequences.
examples: 'If every company does this, what happens to the industry? To public trust in AI?'

dimensions.name: Principle Application
dimensions.description: Ability to apply ethical principles (transparency, privacy, fairness, accountability, human oversight)

levels.label: None
levels.criteria: No ethical principles considered.
examples: Pure efficiency reasoning with no ethical dimension

levels.label: Intuitive
levels.criteria: Applies principles implicitly without naming them.
examples: 'That doesn't feel right' without being able to articulate why

levels.label: Named
levels.criteria: Applies and names relevant principles.
examples: 'This violates the transparency principle — users should know AI is involved'

levels.label: Balanced
levels.criteria: Weighs competing principles when they conflict.
examples: 'Transparency says disclose, but privacy says protect. Here's how I'd balance them...'

levels.label: Expert
levels.criteria: Critiques limitations of principles themselves and adapts to context.
examples: 'The standard framework doesn't fully address this because it assumes individual agency, but here the decision affects a group'

dimensions.name: Pressure Resilience
dimensions.description: Ability to maintain ethical position under organizational or social pressure

levels.label: Capitulates
levels.criteria: Abandons ethical position when challenged.
examples: 'If my boss says it's fine, I guess it's fine'

levels.label: Uncomfortable
levels.criteria: Expresses discomfort but doesn't propose alternatives.
examples: 'I'm not sure about this but I don't know what else to do'

levels.label: Stands Ground
levels.criteria: Maintains position but may not persuade effectively.
examples: 'I still think we should disclose, even if competitors don't'

levels.label: Proposes Alternatives
levels.criteria: Offers practical compromises that preserve ethical core.
examples: 'I understand the time pressure. Here's a hybrid approach that addresses both the deadline and the ethical concern'

levels.label: Expert
levels.criteria: Reframes the conversation from cost-of-ethics to cost-of-not-ethics.
examples: 'The real risk isn't slowing down — it's what happens when this comes out. Let me show you the liability calculation.'

dimensions.name: Action Commitment
dimensions.description: Whether ethical reasoning leads to specific, actionable commitments

levels.label: None
levels.criteria: No action taken or planned.
examples: Acknowledges the issue but does nothing

levels.label: Vague
levels.criteria: Commits to general improvement without specifics.
examples: 'I'll be more careful next time'

levels.label: Specific
levels.criteria: Commits to a specific personal behavior change.
examples: 'I'll verify every AI citation before including it in my research'

levels.label: Systemic
levels.criteria: Creates processes or tools to prevent the issue.
examples: 'I'll create a verification checklist for my team and add it to our review process'

levels.label: Expert
levels.criteria: Drives organizational or community-level change.
examples: 'I'll propose an AI use policy to the department and offer to lead the drafting committee'
